coredata.dc:title: Polyacrylonitrile/N-doped graphene quantum dots nanocomposite activity as SERS nanosensors for detection of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Saloni
Kumar, Rajesh
Yadav, Ram Manohar
coredata.dc:description: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) sensing effective substrates made of polymer and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have attracted significant interest. SERS substrates are intensively used to detect pollutants/hazardous materials in various forms. In this study, we have integrated N-GQDs into polyacrylonitrile (PAN) polymer and fabricated the SERS active substrates, which facilitate the detection of methylene blue (MB). Scanning electron microscopic characterization demonstrated three-dimensional (3D) porous structural/morphology of the composite films. The porous structure formation might be due to solvent evaporation during film fabrication. The goal was to develop biocompatible and low-cost SERS substrates for MB detection. Synthesized 3D porous PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite structure was shown to have an enhancement factor (EF) of 104 that of the polymer surface due to the multiple hotspot resonance effect, excitation dependent emission property and charge transfer induced in the structure. This phenomenon indicates that PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite films have high potential as sensitive and selective nanosensors for the detection of MB. Current research results may lead to new and superior SERS substrates with increased sensitivity and selectivity.

head.ce:title: Polyacrylonitrile/N-doped graphene quantum dots nanocomposite activity as SERS nanosensors for detection of methylene blue

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) sensing effective substrates made of polymer and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have attracted significant interest. SERS substrates are intensively used to detect pollutants/hazardous materials in various forms. In this study, we have integrated N-GQDs into polyacrylonitrile (PAN) polymer and fabricated the SERS active substrates, which facilitate the detection of methylene blue (MB). Scanning electron microscopic characterization demonstrated three-dimensional (3D) porous structural/morphology of the composite films. The porous structure formation might be due to solvent evaporation during film fabrication. The goal was to develop biocompatible and low-cost SERS substrates for MB detection. Synthesized 3D porous PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite structure was shown to have an enhancement factor (EF) of 104 that of the polymer surface due to the multiple hotspot resonance effect, excitation dependent emission property and charge transfer induced in the structure. This phenomenon indicates that PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite films have high potential as sensitive and selective nanosensors for the detection of MB. Current research results may lead to new and superior SERS substrates with increased sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, people have started paying more attention to the purity of their drinking water. The demands of a growing population drive a surge in manufacturing and farming. These Industrial and farming activities have triggered environmental pollution, and its central thread caused declined water quality. According to scientific findings, chemicals from pesticides, drugs, industrial chemicals, agricultural waste (pesticides) etc. are the major contaminants in water. [1–4]. According to the report, industrial dyes used to color paper, silk, wool, and cotton rank as the second biggest cause of water contamination. Chemicals and heavy metals are often found in industrial wastewater [5–11]. An estimated 7 × 108 – 1 × 109 kg of aromatic organic dyes is utilized in the industry each year. Both groundwater and surface water are contaminated by these colors. Additionally, they negatively affect the ecology by preventing photosynthesis in aquatic environments, which put a carcinogenic impact on the food chain owing to bioaccumulation.[12–17].

Several methods have already been implemented by researchers to detect these dyes in aquatic environments [18–24]. Dye detection can be accomplished by a variety of techniques, including ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, calorimetry, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), thin-layer chromatography (TLC), electrochemical approaches, capillary electrophoresis and mass spectrometry (CE-MS) etc [25–39]. However, the complex design of these devices causes physicochemical changes in the water molecules, resulting inaccurate readings. Long-running procedures such as HPLC produce a variety of chemical waste and expose lab personnel to potentially hazardous solvents. Therefore, researchers are attracted to SERS due to the need for a more accessible method [40–50]. The detection limit of SERS can be in the femtomolar and attomolar range, making it a highly sensitive analysis tool [51,52]. Because of its sensitivity, selectivity, harmless procedures and simple sample preparation makes SERS best techniques for sensing and detecting applications.

In numerous study domains, SERS has a plethora of applications and these applications have been widely applied in biomedical [53–55], environmental [56,57], nutrition [58] and other analysis even involving only a single molecule [59–61]. The electromagnetic (EM) hypothesis, which involves the excitation of localized surface plasmons, and the chemical amplification (CE) theory, which explains interactions between molecules, are typically responsible for this occurrence [62–71]. Upon interacting with electromagnetic waves at a certain excitation, metal particles cause collective oscillations of the surface conduction electrons within substrates. These electrons (also known as plasmon) become the most significant contributors to the amplification of the SERS signals when analyte molecules are exposed to the SERS substrate. These signals were strongest around the particles' sharp edges or when two metal nanoparticles were only 10 nm apart from one another. 'Hotspots' or regions of strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) are the result of this phenomenon [58,72,73]. Increased surface charge density at corners and edges creates these hot spots, which can boost intensity by a factor of ∼105.

Direct substrate molecule charge transfer, in which the laser modifies the electron density distribution and, due to increased polarization, improves Raman scattering [62,63]. Carbon nanostructures based SERS substrates have attracted immense interest as a possible solution to explain this and are also cost-effective, biocompatible, and environmentally friendly [74]. Graphene, graphene oxide, and functionalized graphene have been investigated as SERS substrates by researchers [75–77]. However, the observed enhancement factor (EF) is significantly smaller than metal-based SERS substrates.

Nonetheless, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) can be a promising material for SERS substrate due to their quantum confinement, edge effect, photoluminescence [78–81], and hot carrier relaxation [82–84]. These properties have enabled its use in a wide range of technologies, from optoelectronics to catalysis, from biomedicine to sensors. Due to their small size and high surface area, N-GQDs are capable of interacting with a high number of molecules [85].

The loading of nanoparticles onto porous substrates has also been the focus of many SERS detection experiments. [86–88]. These nanoparticles were loaded and tested on several porous structures e.g. porous silicon [89–91] and porous anodic alumina [92–94]. Polymer might be the ideal choice for porous substrates due to its easy manufacture and low cost [95–97]. There are few studies of N-GQDs polymer composites [98]. In the past, polymers were loaded into or sandwiched between nanoparticles/carbon-based structures and employed as SERS substrates [99]. Compared with others, polyacrylonitrile (PAN) has a significantly higher hydrophilicity, which makes it great for this purpose [100]. 3D porous architectures of PAN thin films can ensnare the analyte molecules [101,102].

In this study, we have synthesized PAN/N-GQDs composites and used them as SERS substrate to detect methylene blue (MB) (C16H18ClN3S). N-GQDs plays a key role in the SERS sensitivity of PAN/N-GQDs composite film. Composite films were prepared using PAN fiber with varying percentage of N-GQDs by dip coating techniques on glass substrate. The as grown films have been characterized using various techniques e.g. scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffractometry (XRD), photoluminescence spectra (PL), and ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-Vis). These films have been characterized by Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) to check the MB sensing behavior of composites films.

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) (molecular weight: 15,000) and dimethylformamide (DMF) solution were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and SRL chemicals, respectively. Methylene blue (molecular weight: 373.9) was purchased from FINAR chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were prepared by a simple and cost-effective approach using the hydrothermal method [103]. To synthesize N-GQDs, 3.15 g citric acid (C6H8O7) was dissolved in 30 mL HPLC grade water after that ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) was added into the solution. The mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor and heated at a ramp rate of 1 °C min−1 up to 210 °C and then, temperature was maintained (210 °C) for 24 h. After cooling, powder materials were separated from solution and dried by heating at 50 °C. The collected powder of N-GQDs materials was used for further nanocomposite preparation with PAN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposites and film preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposites and its films fabrication by dip coating method is shown in schematic Fig. 1 . Initially, 10% PAN solution was made by combining 1 g of PAN powder with 10 mL of DMF solvent. The solution was continuously stirred at 50 °C for 2 hrs to ensure the complete dissolving of PAN in solvent. The PAN solution was combined with different wt% of N-GQDs materials for nanocomposite film preparation. In the dip coating process, the glass substrate was submerged for 30 s at a speed of 3000 µm/second in the prepared composite solution. This procedure ensured that the composite solution was uniformly applied to the substrate. The quality of the PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposites films was then increased by heating films at 50 °C for 2 hrs for complete evaporation of DMF solvent.

Crystalline structure of synthesized N-GQDs and PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposites were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD: PANalytical, X′Pert3 Power) with Cu-Kα radiation, λ = 1.540598 Å in the selected 2θ range (2θmax = 50°) at a scanning rate of 0.01°/s. The surface morphology and microstructure of the N-GQDs and nanocomposites was characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM: Carl Zeiss MERLIN VP Compact) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM: JEOL, JSM-2010 F; Talos F200X). The FESEM images were obtained with an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. Raman spectra of the N-GQDs were recorded by Raman spectrometer (WITec alpha 300) with a 532 nm laser. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of N-GQDs materials was carried out by XPS, (PHI Quantera XPS) with Al Kα X-ray source. The operation of these characterization items may refer to the literature [104].

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using Fluorolog HORIBA JOBINYVON, which detects the intensity and wavelength of the light released when a range of wavelengths interact with the sample. An Agilent Cary 5000 equipment was used to conduct UV–visible absorption spectroscopy in the UV to NIR region (200–600 nm)[105]. In order to conduct the SERS experiment, a number of MB solutions in methanol were prepared at concentrations ranging from 1 micro-molar (1 μM) to 5 milli-molar (5 mM). In the SERS experiment, pristine PAN and PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite based films were tested using two micro-liters of each MB dilution using the drop and drying procedure. A confocal microscope with 100X objective captured SERS signals at room temperature. A 532 nm (Nd-YAG-40 mW) excitation laser collected the SERS data, and each measurement had 20 accumulations .

Fig. 2 a shows the SEM image of as synthesized N-GQDs, which show the morphology of aggregated large quantities of N-GQDs. Fig. 2 b shows the HRTEM image of N-GQDs, which shows uniform dispersed particles with a similar diameter of about 4 nm. The creation of N-GQDs exhibits the formation of homogeneous, uniform particles with no size deviations. Fig. 2c shows the HR-TEM image of high quality crystalline spherical N-GQDs. The appearance of lattice fringe patterns is easily visible in HRTEM image. The lattice parameter of N-GQDs was calculated to be ∼0.35 nm from HRTEM image ( Fig. 3c), clearly revealing the high crystalline structure of (002) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon. The XRD pattern of citric acid (with ethylenediamine) derived N-GQDs materials is shown in Fig. 3 a, revealing a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.2° related to (002) plane with d-spacing of 0.353 nm (JCPDS data: 0024)[106,107].

The single peak in the XRD implies that N-GQDs are without the presence of any impurities. Raman spectra were investigated to find out more information about structures of N-GQDs and their connection to the presence of defects. The G-band and D-band at 1345 and 1575 cm−1 are signatures of the graphitic and defects/disordered regions of the N-GQDs material, respectively. The G band of N-GQDs is due to the stretching vibration of the sp2 hybridized carbon bonds in the rings. Destruction of the graphitic lattice is reflected in the D band characteristics as regions of disordered carbon sp3. High intensity of the D band, as shown in Fig. 3 b, confirms the presence of multiple defects and disorders in N-GQDs. This demonstrated that nitrogen doping in GQDs led to major defects or disorder in the graphitic lattice. Reduction in sp2 hybridization of carbon bonds is reflected by a significant decrease in the N-GQDs G band [108,109]. D-band and G-band relative intensities are commonly used to characterize the degree of disorder or graphitization in carbon materials. The calculated intensity ratio of D band to G band i.e., ID/IG of N-GQDs was 2.12 implies the small particle size and high amount of defects formation after N doping, which highly altered the crystalline nature of synthesized N-GQDs. The Raman Spectra of PAN film is shown in FigS1. The peaks in XPS spectra for N1s core level of N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 c. The core level N1s shows four peaks at energies of 398.5 eV, 399.8 eV, 400.2 eV, 401.3 eV, and 402.2 eV when fitted with a Gaussian-Lorentzian distribution. Species of N have been assigned peaks in the ranges of 398.5 eV, 399.8 eV, 400.2 eV, and 401.3, 402.2 eV energy, which correspond to pyridinic N, NH2, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and nitrogen oxide respectively[103,110–113]. It demonstrates that N doping in GQDs is feasible at various sites.

The surface morphology of PAN and PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposites were investigated by SEM characterization, as shown in Fig. 4. PAN and PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite contains cavities of micron-size length responsible for porous structure formation. Although there are some visual similarities between the PAN and PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite images, the brightness of the N-GQDs containing PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite is distinguishable. In SEM images of PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite (Fig. 4 b-d), it is assumed that all N-GQDs materials were covered with the polymer matrix, thus not appearing on the surfaces. XRD pattern of PAN and PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposites are illustrated in Fig. 5. High intensity diffraction peak at 2θ = 17.1° represents the (100) crystal plane of PAN [114,115]. The inclusion of N-GQDs materials in PAN reduces the diffraction peak intensity. The diffraction peak intensity becomes weaker as the high content of N-GQDs is incorporated in PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite. This suggests that the incorporation of defect containing N-GQDs decreases the crystallinity of nanocomposites, and a portion of PANs crystalline area was converted to less crystalline phase.

Photoluminescence spectra of PAN and PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite were collected in the range of 325–550 nm ( Fig. 6 a). It can be seen that after inclusion of N-GQDs in PAN, the photoluminescence intensity was increased, and it was shifted towards high wavelength side in the visible range. The photoluminescence intensity of PAN film was found to be at a wavelength of 398 nm; however, after the inclusion of 2 wt% N-GQDs, the peak shifted towards the visible range to 416 nm with enhanced intensity. A slight decrease was also observed in intensity after increasing the N-GQDs concentration from 2 wt% to 5 wt%, which is expected due to the N-GQDs agglomerations. The increase in intensity and shift in PAN/N-GQDs-2% nanocomposite could be due to enhanced edge emissions at the pore corners. The intensity shift has been confirmed by absorbance data collected with UV visible spectroscopy (Fig. 6 b).

The introduction of N-GQDs into the PAN composite film may have caused a change in intensity for a number of reasons. Intensity shifts follow changes in morphology, crystallinity, and the material's structural and electrical properties. The maximum absorbance was found at 2 wt% of N-GQDs; hence this concentration was chosen for use in the PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite SERS substrate for its superiority in dye detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface-enhanced Raman spectra (SERS) from methylene blue (MB)
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-based materials have been applied as SERS substrates and it shows charge-transfer (CT) dominating mechanics [60]. Due to the absence of plasmons in the N-GQDs, an electromagnetic mechanism (EM) cannot be applied. Fig. 7 a shows the schematic representation of the process of collecting SERS signals. Due to high photoluminescence properties with 2 wt% N-GQDs in PAN, we have used PAN/N-GQDs-2% nanocomposite as SERS substrates. PAN/N-GQDs-2% nanocomposite films were exposed to the various concentrations of targeted dye molecules of MB, and SERS spectra were collected. Fig. 7 b reveals that an enhanced MB peak was found at 1622 cm−1 when 5uM MB was dropped and dried on a substrate which was later intensified with a higher concentration (50uM) of the analyte. The MB dye molecule adsorbs onto the substrate molecule as a chemical process. The fluorescence quenching effect may be responsible for this apparent intensity increase. Due to the charge-transfer (CT) process, the resonant coupling produced by the N-GQDs is more accessible at the edges and surface defects (caused by the H+ intercalation). These defects would create a more active site availability for MB detection. In these PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite films, the hot spot resonance effect is also visible, and due to the multilayer porosity, composite films displayed enhanced SERS signals. In contrast, pristine PAN film shows less sensitivity towards analytes.

Therefore, PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite films are a viable option for a SERS detector. The enhancement factor (EF) can be calculated by.
(1) EF=(ISERS/IRaman)(Nb/Nads)
where ISERS: signal intensity of SERS spectra, IRaman: signal intensity of normal Raman spectra, Nb: number of molecules on a bulk sample and Nads: number of probed molecules contributing to SERS. The EF of 104 was measured for MB detection. The SERS spectra enhancement factor indicates the magnification of the Raman signal of the molecule interacting with the surface of the SERS substrate.

In this research, we have investigated the feasibility of PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposites as SERS substrate to detect MB in water contaminants. To examine the impacts of N-GQDs inclusion into PAN, analyte (MB) SERS spectra were obtained for pristine PAN and PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite thin films at various loading of N-GQDs. The SERS signal for MB was enhanced by a factor of 104 at 1622 cm−1, when 2% N-GQDs were present in PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposite thin films. This increase was due to the proliferation of charge transfer molecules between the MB and the N-GQDs in the PAN matrix. This chemical approach can greatly improve the Raman signal and this study demonstrates that optimized PAN/N-GQDs nanocomposites thin films can function as reliable SERS substrates for MB detection and open the possibility for the detection of other contaminants. This investigation demonstrates the importance of exploring complementary approaches for improving SERS signals. Although up to now the reported sensitivity of the carbon nanostructures based composites are lower than the metal based substrates, but they have more interests due to scarcity and toxicity of SERS sensitive metals and therefore further improvement and optimization of carbon nanostructure based composite for SERS needs to be analyzed and investigated. These carbon-based SERS substrates are less-toxic than metals and would be a better replacement for metal based SERS. Our results suggest that N-GQDS based composites would be futuristic materials for SERS applications.

Saloni Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Rajesh Kumar: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Ram Manohar Yadav: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
